properties.name: 1998 Yangtze River Floods
properties.description: Devastating floods along the Yangtze River affected 223 million people across 29 provinces. The disaster caused $26 billion in damage and prompted major flood control reforms including reforestation and dam construction.
properties.source: EM-DAT; China Ministry of Water Resources

properties.name: 2016 Yangtze River Floods
properties.description: Heavy monsoon rains caused severe flooding along the Yangtze and its tributaries, affecting over 60 million people. Economic losses exceeded $22 billion with massive infrastructure damage.

properties.name: 2020 Yangtze River Floods
properties.description: Record rainfall caused the worst Yangtze flooding since 1998, with floodwaters testing the Three Gorges Dam's capacity. Over 63 million people were affected across central and southern China.

properties.name: 2010 Pakistan Floods
properties.description: Exceptional monsoon rains caused the Indus River to breach its banks, submerging approximately one-fifth of Pakistan. Over 20 million people were affected, making it one of the worst natural disasters in Pakistan's history.

properties.name: 2022 Pakistan Floods
properties.description: Unprecedented monsoon rains and glacial melt submerged a third of Pakistan, displacing 33 million people. The floods caused $30 billion in damage, with scientists linking the extreme rainfall to climate change.

properties.name: 2007 South Asian Floods
properties.description: Extraordinary monsoon flooding affected India, Bangladesh, and Nepal simultaneously, displacing over 30 million people. The Ganges and Brahmaputra rivers reached record levels, inundating vast agricultural regions.

properties.name: 2019 India Monsoon Floods
properties.severity: Severe — rivers overflowed across northern India
properties.description: Heavy monsoon rains caused catastrophic flooding across northern India, particularly in Bihar and Uttar Pradesh. Over 25 million people were affected with massive crop losses.

properties.name: Great Flood of 1993
properties.description: The Great Flood of 1993 was among the most costly and devastating floods in US history. The Mississippi and Missouri rivers flooded for months, inundating 30,000 square miles across nine states.

properties.name: 2011 Mississippi River Floods
properties.severity: Major — record crests along entire river
properties.description: Record flooding along the Mississippi River from Memphis to the Gulf. The Morganza and Birds Point spillways were opened for the first time in decades to protect major cities.

properties.name: 2019 Mississippi River Floods
properties.description: The Mississippi River experienced its longest flood duration on record at some gauges, remaining above flood stage for over 200 days in some locations. The flooding devastated agriculture across the Midwest.

properties.name: 2002 European Floods
properties.severity: Catastrophic — century flood across Central Europe
properties.description: Record-breaking floods struck Central Europe along the Danube and Elbe rivers, with Dresden and Prague severely affected. Damages exceeded €15 billion, prompting EU-wide flood management reforms.

properties.name: 2013 Central European Floods
properties.description: Extensive flooding across Central Europe with the Danube reaching record levels in several locations. Germany, Austria, Czech Republic, and Hungary were severely affected with damages exceeding €12 billion.

properties.name: 1995 Rhine River Floods
properties.description: Major flooding along the Rhine and Meuse rivers forced the evacuation of 250,000 people in the Netherlands. Dikes came dangerously close to failing, prompting a massive national flood defense program.

properties.name: 2021 Western European Floods
properties.severity: Catastrophic — deadliest European flood in decades
properties.description: Extreme rainfall caused catastrophic flash flooding in the Ahr Valley and surrounding regions. The disaster killed nearly 200 people in Germany and Belgium, exposing gaps in flood warning systems.

properties.name: 2009 Amazon Basin Floods
properties.description: The Amazon River and its tributaries reached record levels, displacing hundreds of thousands across Brazil's northern states. The flooding destroyed crops and contaminated water supplies.

properties.name: 2021 Amazon Floods
properties.severity: Major — historic flood levels at Manaus
properties.description: The Negro River at Manaus reached its highest level ever recorded, flooding large parts of the city and surrounding communities. Over 450,000 people were affected across the Amazon basin.

properties.name: 2000 Mekong River Floods
properties.description: The worst Mekong flooding in four decades affected Cambodia, Vietnam, Laos, and Thailand. Over 5 million people were affected with massive damage to rice crops and infrastructure.

properties.name: 2011 Southeast Asian Floods
properties.description: Extensive flooding along the Mekong River affected Cambodia, Laos, Vietnam, and Thailand simultaneously. Over 9 million people were affected across the region.

properties.name: 2020 Sudan Nile Floods
properties.description: The Blue Nile reached its highest recorded level at Khartoum, causing devastating floods across Sudan. Over 875,000 people were affected and 100,000 homes destroyed.

properties.name: 2024 Sudan Floods
properties.description: Devastating floods compounded Sudan's humanitarian crisis amid ongoing civil war. The Nile and its tributaries overwhelmed infrastructure, displacing hundreds of thousands in an already displaced population.

properties.name: 2023 Libya Flash Floods (Derna)
properties.description: Storm Daniel caused catastrophic flooding in Derna when two dams collapsed, sending a wall of water through the city. Entire neighborhoods were swept into the sea, with over 11,000 confirmed dead and thousands still missing.

properties.name: 2018 Kerala Floods
properties.description: Kerala experienced its worst flooding in nearly a century as unprecedented monsoon rains and dam releases inundated the state. Over 5 million people were displaced and one-sixth of the population was directly affected.
properties.source: EM-DAT; IMD; Kerala State Disaster Management

properties.name: 2013 Uttarakhand Flash Floods
properties.description: Extremely heavy rainfall triggered massive flash floods and landslides in Uttarakhand, devastating the holy town of Kedarnath. The disaster, called a 'Himalayan tsunami,' killed thousands of pilgrims and residents.

properties.name: 1999 Vargas Tragedy
properties.description: Torrential rains triggered catastrophic flash floods and mudslides along Venezuela's Caribbean coast. Entire communities were buried or washed away in what became the deadliest natural disaster in Venezuelan history.

properties.name: 1988 Bangladesh Floods
properties.description: The most severe flooding in Bangladesh's history submerged approximately two-thirds of the country for weeks. Over 45 million people were left homeless and critical infrastructure was devastated.

properties.name: 1998 Bangladesh Floods
properties.description: Prolonged monsoon flooding submerged 75% of Bangladesh for over two months. Over 30 million people were displaced, with devastating impacts on agriculture and infrastructure.

properties.name: 2004 Bangladesh Floods
properties.description: Heavy monsoon rains caused severe flooding across Bangladesh, affecting over 36 million people. The floods destroyed 2.4 million homes and caused massive agricultural losses.

properties.name: 2017 Bangladesh Floods
properties.description: Monsoon flooding affected a third of Bangladesh, displacing over 8 million people. The floods coincided with a Rohingya refugee crisis, compounding the humanitarian situation.

properties.name: 2020 Bangladesh Monsoon Floods
properties.description: Intense monsoon rains and upstream water from India submerged a quarter of Bangladesh. Over 5.4 million people were affected, with major damage to crops and infrastructure.

properties.name: 2005 Maharashtra Floods
properties.description: Mumbai received 944mm of rain in 24 hours — an Indian record — paralyzing the city and killing over 1,000 people. The flooding exposed the vulnerability of India's financial capital to extreme weather.

properties.name: 2014 India-Pakistan Floods (Kashmir)
properties.description: Devastating floods hit the Kashmir Valley after heavy monsoon rains, submerging much of Srinagar. Over 2 million people were affected in India and Pakistan combined.

properties.name: 2023 North India Floods
properties.description: Exceptionally heavy monsoon rains triggered flash floods and landslides across northern India. Himachal Pradesh was hardest hit with widespread infrastructure destruction and hundreds dead.

properties.name: 2011 Thailand Floods
properties.description: Thailand's worst flooding in half a century inundated central Thailand for months, including parts of Bangkok. The disaster disrupted global supply chains, particularly hard drive manufacturing, causing $46 billion in damage.

properties.name: Hurricane Harvey Flooding
properties.description: Hurricane Harvey stalled over Houston, dumping over 1.5 meters of rain over four days. The unprecedented flooding submerged entire neighborhoods, damaged 300,000 structures, and caused $125 billion in damage.

properties.name: 2024 Dubai Floods
properties.severity: Severe — record rainfall in desert city
properties.description: The UAE received nearly two years' worth of rain in a single day, causing unprecedented flooding across Dubai. The desert city's infrastructure was overwhelmed, stranding thousands and disrupting global air travel.

properties.name: 2007 Jakarta Floods
properties.description: Heavy rains and overflowing rivers inundated 60% of Jakarta, displacing over 340,000 people. The flooding highlighted Jakarta's chronic vulnerability to flooding due to land subsidence and poor drainage.

properties.name: 2020 Jakarta New Year Floods
properties.severity: Major — deadly start to new year
properties.description: Extreme rainfall on New Year's Eve 2019 triggered deadly floods and landslides across Jakarta. Tens of thousands were displaced as the city experienced its worst flooding since 2013.

properties.name: 2010-11 Queensland Floods
properties.severity: Catastrophic — area larger than France flooded
properties.description: Massive flooding affected an area larger than France and Germany combined across Queensland. The city of Brisbane was severely impacted, and the Lockyer Valley experienced devastating flash floods.

properties.name: 2022 Eastern Australia Floods
properties.description: Eastern Australia experienced repeated catastrophic flooding events throughout 2022, with Lismore flooded twice. The disasters were linked to a prolonged La Niña pattern and climate change.

properties.name: 2022 Seoul Floods
properties.description: Record-breaking rainfall of over 140mm per hour inundated parts of Seoul, including the Gangnam district. Semi-basement apartments were flooded, trapping and killing residents in scenes reminiscent of the film Parasite.

properties.name: 2018 Japan Floods (Western)
properties.description: Record-breaking rainfall caused devastating floods and landslides across western Japan, killing over 200 people. Hiroshima and Okayama prefectures were hardest hit with entire communities inundated.

properties.name: 2020 Kyushu Floods
properties.description: Torrential rain caused severe flooding along the Kuma River in Kumamoto Prefecture. Elderly care facilities were inundated and entire riverside communities were devastated.

properties.name: 1975-76 Banqiao Dam Aftermath
properties.description: The aftermath of the 1975 Banqiao Dam failure continued into 1976, with ongoing displacement and disease. The disaster, caused by Typhoon Nina, killed an estimated 26,000-240,000 people and destroyed 6.8 million homes.

properties.name: 2021 Henan Floods
properties.description: Extreme rainfall dumped a year's worth of rain on Zhengzhou in three days, causing catastrophic urban flooding. Subway tunnels were inundated, trapping commuters, in one of China's deadliest urban flood events.

properties.name: 1999 Vietnam Central Coast Floods
properties.description: Devastating floods struck Vietnam's central provinces, with Hue city particularly hard hit. The flooding was described as the worst in a century, destroying hundreds of thousands of homes.

properties.name: 2020 Vietnam Central Floods
properties.description: A series of tropical storms brought devastating floods and landslides to central Vietnam over several weeks. Military barracks and remote villages were buried by landslides.

properties.name: 2013 Cambodia Floods
properties.description: Prolonged flooding across Cambodia's central lowlands affected over 1.7 million people. The floods devastated the rice harvest, causing severe food security concerns.

properties.name: 2015 Myanmar Floods
properties.description: Cyclone Komen combined with monsoon rains caused devastating flooding across Myanmar, affecting over 9 million people. The Irrawaddy Delta and Chin State were among the hardest hit regions.

properties.name: 2012 Nigeria Floods
properties.description: Nigeria experienced its worst flooding in half a century as the Niger and Benue rivers overflowed. Over 2 million people were displaced and 600,000 homes were destroyed across 30 states.

properties.name: 2022 Nigeria Floods
properties.description: Nigeria experienced its worst floods on record, surpassing the devastating 2012 event. Over 2.5 million people were displaced, with Bayelsa and Anambra states among the worst affected.

properties.name: 2013 Sudan Floods
properties.description: Heavy rains and Nile flooding displaced over 300,000 people across Sudan. Khartoum and surrounding areas were heavily affected with thousands of homes destroyed.

properties.name: 2000 Mozambique Floods
properties.description: Cyclone Eline caused catastrophic flooding in Mozambique, with images of people stranded on rooftops and trees capturing world attention. The Limpopo and Save rivers reached record levels, devastating the south.

properties.name: 2019 Cyclone Idai Floods
properties.severity: Catastrophic — city devastated by cyclone and floods
properties.description: Cyclone Idai struck Beira and caused massive inland flooding in Mozambique, Zimbabwe, and Malawi. The city of Beira was 90% destroyed, and an inland sea formed that was visible from space.

properties.name: 2007 United Kingdom Floods
properties.description: Exceptionally heavy summer rainfall caused widespread flooding across England, particularly in Yorkshire, the Midlands, and Gloucestershire. Over 55,000 properties were flooded and 7,000 people were rescued.

properties.name: 2015 Storm Desmond UK Floods
properties.description: Storm Desmond brought record-breaking rainfall to Cumbria, with over 340mm in 24 hours. Severe flooding devastated towns across northern England, particularly Carlisle and Cockermouth.

properties.name: 2023 Emilia-Romagna Floods
properties.description: Two rounds of extreme rainfall within weeks caused catastrophic flooding across Emilia-Romagna. Over 36,000 people were evacuated and 23 rivers overflowed in what authorities called an unprecedented disaster.

properties.name: 2024 Valencia Floods (DANA)
properties.severity: Catastrophic — deadliest European flood in decades
properties.description: A DANA weather system dumped extreme rainfall on Valencia, causing devastating flash floods that swept through towns south of the city. It became Spain's deadliest flood disaster in modern history.

properties.name: 2011 Rio de Janeiro Floods
properties.description: Torrential rains triggered devastating mudslides and floods in Rio de Janeiro's mountain region. Seven towns were struck by walls of mud, making it the deadliest natural disaster in Brazilian history.

properties.name: 2024 Rio Grande do Sul Floods
properties.description: Record-breaking rainfall caused catastrophic flooding across Rio Grande do Sul, including the state capital Porto Alegre. Over 2 million people were affected in what was described as the state's worst disaster ever.

properties.name: 2002 China Floods
properties.description: Heavy monsoon rains caused widespread flooding across southern China, with Hunan and Jiangxi provinces severely affected. Over 100 million people were impacted with significant agricultural losses.

properties.name: 2010 China Southern Floods
properties.description: Persistent heavy rains caused severe flooding across southern China, with Fujian, Jiangxi, and Guangdong provinces hardest hit. Over 134 million people were affected.

properties.name: 1976 Pakistan Floods
properties.description: Heavy monsoon rains caused the Indus River and its tributaries to flood extensively across Punjab and Sindh. Millions of acres of farmland were submerged.

properties.name: 2008 Cyclone Nargis Flooding
properties.description: Cyclone Nargis caused catastrophic storm surge flooding across Myanmar's Irrawaddy Delta. Over 138,000 people died in one of the deadliest natural disasters in recorded history.

properties.name: 2014 Balkans Floods
properties.description: Cyclone Tamara caused the worst flooding in the Balkans in 120 years, affecting Serbia, Bosnia, and Croatia. Landslides unearthed landmines from the 1990s Balkan Wars.

properties.name: 2016 Louisiana Floods
properties.description: An unnamed storm dumped over 600mm of rain on Louisiana over three days, causing catastrophic flooding in Baton Rouge and surrounding areas. Over 140,000 homes were damaged.

properties.name: 2017 Nepal Terai Floods
properties.description: Massive monsoon flooding affected the southern Terai region, displacing 1.7 million people. Over 35 districts were affected, making it one of Nepal's worst flood disasters.

properties.name: 1997 East Africa Floods (El Niño)
properties.description: Extreme El Niño rains caused devastating flooding across the Horn of Africa, with Somalia and Kenya hardest hit. The Juba and Shabelle rivers overflowed for weeks, destroying livelihoods.

properties.name: 1991 Eastern China Floods
properties.description: Severe flooding along the Yangtze and Huai rivers devastated eastern China. Over 200 million people were affected, with Anhui Province bearing the brunt of the disaster.

properties.name: 2025 Brazil Northeast Floods
properties.description: Torrential rains caused severe flooding across Bahia state, displacing tens of thousands. Multiple rivers overflowed their banks, destroying bridges and isolating communities.

properties.name: 1978 North India Floods
properties.description: Severe monsoon flooding along the Ganges and its tributaries devastated northern India. Millions were displaced across Uttar Pradesh and Bihar with extensive crop losses.

properties.name: 2025 Palisades Fire
properties.severity: Catastrophic — destroyed thousands of structures in LA
properties.description: The Palisades Fire tore through Pacific Palisades and surrounding areas of Los Angeles during extreme Santa Ana winds. Thousands of homes and businesses were destroyed in one of the most destructive urban wildfires in US history.

properties.name: 2025 Eaton Fire
properties.description: The Eaton Fire ravaged the community of Altadena near Pasadena, destroying hundreds of homes. Combined with the simultaneous Palisades Fire, Los Angeles faced an unprecedented wildfire crisis.

properties.name: 2024 Park Fire
properties.severity: Major — one of largest CA fires ever
properties.description: The Park Fire became one of the largest wildfires in California history, burning over 429,000 acres in Butte and Tehama counties. The fire was allegedly started by arson.

properties.name: 2023 Maui Wildfires (Lahaina)
properties.severity: Catastrophic — deadliest US wildfire in century
properties.description: Wind-driven wildfires devastated the historic town of Lahaina on Maui, killing 100 people and destroying over 2,200 structures. It was the deadliest US wildfire in over a century.

properties.name: 2021 Dixie Fire
properties.severity: Major — largest single fire in CA history
properties.description: The Dixie Fire became California's largest single wildfire, burning 963,309 acres across five counties. The fire destroyed the town of Greenville and burned for over three months.

properties.name: 2020 August Complex Fire
properties.severity: Major — largest fire in CA history at the time
properties.description: The August Complex Fire became California's first gigafire, burning over 1 million acres. It was a complex of 38 fires ignited by lightning in the Mendocino National Forest.

properties.name: 2020 Creek Fire
properties.description: The Creek Fire generated a rare fire tornado and pyrocumulonimbus cloud, creating extreme fire behavior. Over 850 structures were destroyed and dozens of people were rescued by military helicopters from Mammoth Pool.

properties.name: 2019 Kincade Fire
properties.severity: Major — massive evacuation in wine country
properties.description: The Kincade Fire forced the largest evacuation in Sonoma County history, with nearly 200,000 people ordered to leave. The fire burned 77,758 acres in California's wine country.

properties.name: 2018 Camp Fire
properties.severity: Catastrophic — deadliest & most destructive CA fire
properties.description: The Camp Fire destroyed the town of Paradise in hours, killing 85 people and destroying 18,804 structures. It remains California's deadliest and most destructive wildfire.

properties.name: 2018 Woolsey Fire
properties.description: The Woolsey Fire burned nearly 97,000 acres from Simi Valley to Malibu, destroying 1,600 structures. The fire burned through the Santa Monica Mountains to the Pacific Ocean.

properties.name: 2017 Thomas Fire
properties.region: Ventura and Santa Barbara Counties, CA
properties.description: The Thomas Fire became the largest wildfire in California's modern history at the time, burning 281,893 acres. Subsequent debris flows in Montecito killed 23 people.

properties.name: 2017 Tubbs Fire
properties.description: The Tubbs Fire raced through suburban Santa Rosa with devastating speed, destroying entire neighborhoods including Coffey Park. Over 5,600 structures were destroyed.

properties.name: 2016 Fort McMurray Wildfire
properties.severity: Catastrophic — largest evacuation in Alberta history
properties.description: The Horse River wildfire forced the evacuation of all 88,000 residents of Fort McMurray. The fire burned 590,000 hectares and caused $9.9 billion in damage, Canada's costliest disaster.
properties.source: Alberta Wildfire; Insurance Bureau of Canada

properties.name: 2015 Valley Fire
properties.description: The Valley Fire spread with explosive speed through Lake County, destroying 1,955 structures. The fire burned 76,067 acres and was one of the most destructive fires in California history.

properties.name: 2014 Carlton Complex Fire
properties.severity: Major — largest fire in Washington state history
properties.description: The Carlton Complex Fire became the largest wildfire in Washington state history, burning 256,108 acres. Over 300 homes were destroyed in the rural Methow Valley region.

properties.name: 2013 Rim Fire
properties.description: The Rim Fire burned 257,314 acres near and inside Yosemite National Park. The fire threatened San Francisco's water supply from the Hetch Hetchy Reservoir.

properties.name: 2012 Waldo Canyon Fire
properties.description: The Waldo Canyon Fire burned into Colorado Springs, destroying 346 homes in the Mountain Shadows neighborhood. It was the most destructive fire in Colorado history at the time.

properties.name: 2011 Bastrop County Complex Fire
properties.description: The Bastrop County Complex Fire destroyed 1,645 homes, making it the most destructive wildfire in Texas history. Extreme drought and high winds drove the fire through pine forests east of Austin.

properties.name: 2007 Witch Fire
properties.severity: Major — part of devastating fire siege
properties.description: The Witch Fire was the largest of several wildfires during the October 2007 Southern California fire siege. Over 500,000 people were evacuated in the largest evacuation in California history.

properties.name: 2003 Cedar Fire
properties.description: The Cedar Fire burned 273,246 acres and destroyed 2,820 buildings, making it the largest and most destructive fire in California history at the time. The fire was started by a lost hunter's signal fire.

properties.name: 2000 Cerro Grande Fire
properties.description: A prescribed burn escaped and burned into Los Alamos, destroying 235 homes and forcing evacuation of the Los Alamos National Laboratory. The incident led to major reforms in prescribed fire policy.

properties.name: 2019-20 Australian Black Summer
properties.description: The Black Summer bushfires burned over 46 million acres across southeastern Australia, killing 34 people and an estimated 3 billion animals. The fires generated their own weather systems and darkened New Zealand's skies.

properties.name: 2009 Black Saturday Bushfires
properties.description: The Black Saturday bushfires killed 173 people on February 7, 2009, during record heat and strong winds. The fires destroyed 2,029 homes and burned over 450,000 hectares across Victoria.

properties.name: 2003 Canberra Bushfires
properties.description: Bushfires entered Canberra's suburbs during extreme fire weather, destroying 488 homes. The fires were the worst to affect any Australian capital city and killed four people.

properties.name: 1983 Ash Wednesday Bushfires
properties.description: Over 180 fires burned across Victoria and South Australia on Ash Wednesday, killing 75 people. The fires destroyed over 2,000 homes in conditions of extreme heat, low humidity, and strong winds.
properties.source: CFA; CFS; Australian Institute for Disaster Resilience

properties.name: 2023 Evros Wildfire (Greece)
properties.severity: Major — largest fire in EU history
properties.description: The Evros wildfire became the largest ever recorded in the European Union, burning over 96,000 hectares. The fire burned for weeks in the Dadia Forest near the Turkish border.

properties.name: 2023 Rhodes Wildfire
properties.description: A massive wildfire on the Greek island of Rhodes forced the evacuation of 19,000 tourists in the largest evacuation in Greek history. Hotels and beaches were engulfed as the fire burned across the island.

properties.name: 2022 Gironde Wildfires (France)
properties.severity: Major — record fires in southwest France
properties.description: Two major wildfires in the Gironde department burned over 30,000 hectares of pine forest near Bordeaux. Over 36,000 people were evacuated in France's worst wildfire season in recent history.

properties.name: 2022 Zamora Wildfire (Spain)
properties.severity: Major — largest single fire in Spanish history
properties.description: The Sierra de la Culebra fire in Zamora province became the largest single wildfire in Spanish history, burning over 30,000 hectares. The fire was part of Spain's devastating 2022 fire season.

properties.name: 2021 Evia Island Wildfire (Greece)
properties.description: A massive wildfire burned across the northern part of Evia island for over two weeks, destroying 50,000 hectares of forest. Thousands of residents were evacuated by sea.

properties.name: 2021 Turkey Wildfires (Manavgat)
properties.severity: Major — worst fires in Turkish history
properties.description: Turkey experienced its worst wildfire season on record, with over 200 fires burning simultaneously. The Manavgat fire near Antalya was among the most devastating, destroying villages and killing 8 people.

properties.name: 2018 Attica Wildfire (Greece)
properties.description: A fast-moving wildfire swept through the coastal town of Mati near Athens, trapping residents against the sea. 104 people died in Greece's deadliest wildfire, many found in groups trying to reach the beach.
properties.source: Greek Fire Service; Hellenic Parliament Inquiry

properties.name: 2017 Pedrógão Grande Fire (Portugal)
properties.description: A devastating wildfire near Pedrógão Grande killed 66 people, many trapped in their cars on a road between villages. The disaster exposed failures in Portugal's fire warning and response systems.

properties.name: 2007 Greek Wildfires
properties.description: Over 3,000 fires burned across Greece during the catastrophic 2007 fire season, killing 84 people. The Peloponnese was hardest hit, with entire villages destroyed and ancient Olympia threatened.
properties.source: Greek Fire Service; EU Civil Protection

properties.name: 2019 Amazon Rainforest Fires
properties.severity: Severe — international outcry over deforestation fires
properties.description: A dramatic increase in Amazon fires in 2019 triggered international outrage and diplomatic tensions. Over 80,000 fires were detected, many linked to illegal deforestation for cattle ranching.

properties.name: 2020 Pantanal Fires (Brazil)
properties.severity: Catastrophic — worst fires in world's largest wetland
properties.description: Record fires burned over 4 million hectares of the Pantanal, the world's largest tropical wetland. An estimated 17 million vertebrate animals were killed in the ecological catastrophe.

properties.name: 2015 Indonesian Forest Fires
properties.severity: Catastrophic — toxic haze across Southeast Asia
properties.description: Massive peat and forest fires created a toxic haze that blanketed Southeast Asia for months. The fires released more CO2 daily than the entire US economy and caused an estimated $16 billion in damage.

properties.name: 2019 Indonesian Forest Fires
properties.description: Dry conditions and agricultural burning triggered widespread fires across Sumatra and Kalimantan, sending toxic haze across Malaysia and Singapore. Nearly 900,000 people suffered respiratory problems.

properties.name: 2021 Siberian Wildfires
properties.description: Unprecedented wildfires burned over 17 million hectares across Siberia, blanketing cities in toxic smoke. The fires were fueled by record heat in the Arctic and burning permafrost peatlands.

properties.name: 2019 Siberian Wildfires
properties.description: Massive wildfires burned across Siberia, with smoke covering an area larger than the European Union. The fires contributed to Arctic warming through black carbon deposition on ice.

properties.name: 2023 Canadian Wildfires
properties.severity: Catastrophic — worst fire season in Canadian history
properties.description: Canada experienced its worst wildfire season ever, with over 18 million hectares burned — more than double the previous record. Smoke blanketed much of North America, turning New York City's skies orange.

properties.name: 2017 British Columbia Wildfires
properties.severity: Major — worst BC fire season at the time
properties.description: British Columbia experienced its worst fire season on record at the time, with over 1.2 million hectares burned. 65,000 people were evacuated, including the entire city of Williams Lake.

properties.name: 2017 Knysna Fires (South Africa)
properties.description: Devastating wildfires driven by extreme winds swept through the Knysna area along South Africa's Garden Route. Over 1,000 structures were destroyed in the worst wildfire disaster in South African history.
properties.source: Working on Fire; Garden Route DM

properties.name: 2017 Chile Wildfires
properties.severity: Catastrophic — worst fires in Chilean history
properties.description: Chile's worst wildfire disaster burned over 500,000 hectares across central Chile. The fires destroyed the town of Santa Olga entirely and overwhelmed the country's firefighting capacity.

properties.name: 2023 Chile Wildfires (Viña del Mar)
properties.description: Devastating wildfires struck the Valparaíso region, burning into the outskirts of Viña del Mar and killing over 130 people. The fires were among the deadliest in Chilean history.

properties.name: 2016 Saskatchewan Wildfires
properties.description: Major wildfires burned across Saskatchewan, forcing the evacuation of La Ronge and surrounding communities. Over 1.8 million hectares burned in the province's severe 2015-16 fire seasons.

properties.name: 2024 Jasper Wildfire
properties.severity: Catastrophic — destroyed parts of Jasper townsite
properties.description: A devastating wildfire burned into the Jasper townsite in Jasper National Park, destroying approximately one-third of the town's structures. All 25,000 residents and visitors were evacuated.

properties.name: 2015 Siberian Wildfires
properties.severity: Major — extensive burning across eastern Siberia
properties.description: Large-scale wildfires burned across eastern Siberia's Transbaikal region, killing 34 people and destroying over 1,200 homes. Agricultural burning and dry conditions fueled the fires.

properties.name: 2016 Israel Haifa Fires
properties.description: Wildfires driven by extreme heat and dry conditions forced the evacuation of 80,000 residents of Haifa. Israel suspected arson and requested international firefighting assistance.

properties.name: 2018 Sweden Wildfires
properties.description: Sweden experienced its worst wildfire season in modern history as record heat and drought dried out forests. Multiple EU countries sent firefighting assistance in an unprecedented response.

properties.name: 2022 Algeria Wildfires
properties.severity: Major — deadly fires in northern Algeria
properties.description: Devastating wildfires swept through the Kabylie region of northern Algeria, killing 43 people including 25 soldiers deployed to fight the fires. Dozens of villages were destroyed.

properties.name: 2020 Cameron Peak Fire
properties.severity: Major — largest fire in Colorado history
properties.description: The Cameron Peak Fire became the largest wildfire in Colorado history, burning 208,913 acres west of Fort Collins. The fire burned for months and was followed by destructive post-fire flooding.

properties.name: 2022 Hermits Peak/Calf Canyon Fire
properties.severity: Major — largest fire in NM history, started by USFS
properties.description: The Hermits Peak/Calf Canyon Fire became the largest wildfire in New Mexico history, burning 341,471 acres. The fire was started by escaped USFS prescribed burns, leading to a federal compensation program.

properties.name: 2024 Smokehouse Creek Fire
properties.severity: Major — largest fire in Texas history
properties.description: The Smokehouse Creek Fire became the largest wildfire in Texas history, burning over 1 million acres across the Texas Panhandle. The fire killed 2 people and destroyed cattle herds across multiple ranches.

properties.name: 2011 Horn of Africa Drought
properties.description: Severe drought across the Horn of Africa led to famine being declared in parts of Somalia. Over 13 million people were affected across Somalia, Kenya, Ethiopia, and Djibouti.

properties.name: 2017 Horn of Africa Drought
properties.description: Prolonged drought across East Africa left over 20 million people facing food insecurity. Pastoral communities lost up to 60% of their livestock in Kenya, Somalia, and Ethiopia.

properties.name: 2022-23 Horn of Africa Drought
properties.severity: Catastrophic — five consecutive failed rainy seasons
properties.description: An unprecedented five consecutive failed rainy seasons caused the worst drought in 40 years across the Horn of Africa. Over 36 million people were affected, with near-famine conditions in Somalia.

properties.name: 1992 Southern Africa Drought
properties.description: The worst drought in a century struck southern Africa, devastating agriculture across the region. Maize production fell by 60% in Zimbabwe and Zambia, leaving millions dependent on food aid.

properties.name: 2002 Southern Africa Food Crisis
properties.description: Drought combined with economic factors created a severe food crisis across southern Africa. Over 14 million people faced starvation in Malawi, Zimbabwe, Zambia, Mozambique, Swaziland, and Lesotho.

properties.name: 2015-16 Southern Africa Drought (El Niño)
properties.severity: Severe — strongest El Niño in decades
properties.description: The strongest El Niño in decades caused devastating drought across southern Africa. South Africa experienced its driest year since records began in 1904, devastating the maize crop.
properties.source: SADC; FAO; South African Weather Service

properties.name: 2019 Southern Africa Drought
properties.description: Severe drought reduced water levels in the Zambezi River and Kariba Dam to record lows. Zambia and Zimbabwe faced crippling power shortages as hydroelectric generation plummeted.

properties.name: 1987 India Drought
properties.description: India's worst drought of the 20th century affected over 285 million people across the country. Monsoon rainfall was 19% below normal, devastating agriculture and triggering massive relief operations.

properties.name: 2002 India Drought
properties.description: India experienced its worst drought since 1987 as monsoon rainfall was 19% below normal. Over 300 million people were affected, with severe impacts on agriculture and water supply.

properties.name: 2009 India Drought
properties.description: India received its weakest monsoon rainfall in 37 years, with a 23% deficit. Food prices surged and the government had to import sugar for the first time in years.

properties.name: 2015-16 India Drought
properties.description: Two consecutive years of deficient monsoon rainfall caused severe drought across India, particularly in Maharashtra and southern states. Over 330 million people were affected.

properties.name: 2019 India Water Crisis
properties.description: Severe drought and water mismanagement led to an acute water crisis in Chennai and across southern India. Reservoirs ran dry and millions depended on water tankers for basic needs.

properties.name: 1988 US Drought and Heat Wave
properties.description: The worst US drought since the Dust Bowl devastated agriculture across the Great Plains and Midwest. The drought caused $40 billion in damage and led to the creation of the US Drought Monitor.
properties.source: NOAA; USDA; National Drought Mitigation Center

properties.name: 1998 US Southern Drought
properties.description: Severe drought gripped Texas and the southern Plains, causing $6 billion in agricultural losses. Extreme heat accompanied the drought, with temperatures exceeding 110°F for weeks.

properties.name: 1999-2000 US Drought
properties.description: A persistent drought across the eastern and southern US caused billions in agricultural damage. The drought triggered devastating wildfires and severe water restrictions across multiple states.

properties.name: 2012 US Drought
properties.description: The most extensive US drought in over 50 years affected 80% of agricultural land. The drought caused $30 billion in damage and significantly impacted global food prices.

properties.name: 2021 Western US Drought
properties.description: The western US experienced its worst drought in at least 1,200 years, with Lake Mead and Lake Powell reaching record low levels. Water restrictions were imposed across the Colorado River basin for the first time.
properties.source: NOAA; US Drought Monitor; Bureau of Reclamation

properties.name: 2001-09 Millennium Drought (Australia)
properties.description: The Millennium Drought was the worst drought in Australia's recorded history, lasting from 2001 to 2009. Water inflows to the Murray-Darling Basin fell to record lows, devastating agriculture and threatening urban water supplies.

properties.name: 2018-19 Australian Drought
properties.description: Severe drought gripped eastern Australia, particularly New South Wales and Queensland. Farmers faced the worst conditions in living memory, with massive livestock losses and rural mental health crises.

properties.name: 2001 Brazil Energy Crisis (Drought)
properties.description: Severe drought reduced hydroelectric reservoir levels to critical lows, triggering nationwide energy rationing. Brazil was forced to implement mandatory 20% reduction in electricity consumption for months.

properties.name: 2014-15 São Paulo Water Crisis
properties.description: São Paulo experienced its worst drought in 80 years, with the Cantareira reservoir system nearly running dry. Over 20 million people faced water rationing in Brazil's largest city.

properties.name: 2021 Brazil Drought
properties.description: The worst drought in 91 years struck central and southern Brazil, severely impacting hydroelectric generation and agriculture. The Paraná River reached record low levels.

properties.name: 2024 Amazon Drought
properties.description: The Amazon River system experienced unprecedented low water levels, with the Negro River at Manaus reaching its lowest recorded level. Hundreds of river communities were isolated and Amazon dolphins died in overheated shallow waters.

properties.name: 2003 European Heatwave and Drought
properties.description: An unprecedented heatwave combined with severe drought struck Europe, killing over 70,000 people. Agricultural losses exceeded €13 billion and rivers across the continent reached record lows.

properties.name: 2018 European Drought
properties.description: Severe drought affected northern and central Europe from spring through autumn. The Rhine River fell to record lows, disrupting shipping, while agricultural losses reached billions of euros.

properties.name: 2022 European Drought
properties.description: Europe experienced its worst drought in at least 500 years, with rivers across the continent at record lows. The drought exposed sunken ships in the Danube and 'hunger stones' in the Rhine.

properties.name: 2022 China Yangtze Drought
properties.severity: Severe — worst heatwave and drought on record
properties.description: China experienced its worst heatwave and drought on record along the Yangtze River basin. The drought exposed the riverbed of the Yangtze, dried up lakes, and caused severe power shortages in Sichuan.

properties.name: 2018 Afghanistan Drought
properties.description: Severe drought displaced over 250,000 people across northern and western Afghanistan. Crop production fell by 50% and livestock losses were devastating in a country already weakened by conflict.

properties.name: 2014-16 Central American Drought (Dry Corridor)
properties.description: Repeated drought years devastated the Central American Dry Corridor, affecting 3.5 million people. Subsistence farmers lost up to 80% of their maize and bean crops, contributing to migration pressures.

properties.name: 2007-10 Fertile Crescent Drought
properties.description: The worst drought in the Fertile Crescent in 900 years devastated agriculture in Syria, Iraq, and Turkey. In Syria, 1.5 million people migrated to cities, contributing to social tensions preceding the civil war.
